title: Concurrency Foundations
learningObjectives: Explain Concurrency Foundations with advanced interview clarity
Implement Concurrency Foundations in Java with clean design
Avoid high-frequency failure patterns in Concurrency Foundations
Apply this concept to production-oriented scenarios
Communicate decisions with concise technical reasoning

sections.title: Why Concurrency Foundations matters
sections.content:
Concurrency basics are essential to build responsive systems without race-condition failures.

Day 34 is an inflection point between writing code that "works" and writing code that scales in maintainability and production reliability.

Interviewers at this stage evaluate:
1. Internal understanding (how it works, not only syntax)
2. Trade-off reasoning (why this approach over alternatives)
3. Runtime impact (performance, memory, contention, failure behavior)

This topic should be explained with one minimal example, one realistic failure mode, and one mitigation strategy.

If you can defend your design choices clearly, you will stand out from syntax-only answers.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Concurrency Foundations
sections.content:
### Day 34: Concurrency Foundations
Concurrency basics are essential to build responsive systems without race-condition failures.

### Deep understanding goal
You should understand the mechanism, its boundaries, and when to avoid it.

### Practical workflow
1. Implement baseline.
2. Add correctness checks and edge handling.
3. Evaluate runtime cost.
4. Refactor for readability and team adoption.

### Interview quality bar
- Explain what problem it solves.
- Show concise Java example.
- Name one pitfall and one mitigation.
- State complexity/runtime implication.
- Compare with one alternative.

### Production angle
In backend systems, this concept influences throughput, reliability, and debugging speed. A good implementation must support operations, not just local correctness.

### Quick self-check
- Can I explain this in <60 seconds?
- Can I code it from memory?
- Can I describe a failure scenario and fix?

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Annotation processing** powers Lombok, MapStruct, validation — compile-time code generation vs runtime scanning.

**Retention policies:** SOURCE, CLASS, RUNTIME — choose based on who reads them.

**Constraints:** annotations cannot extend other annotations — compose multiple markers instead.

**Testing:** verify generated behaviour with bytecode viewers only when debugging processors — usually trust integration tests.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Thread + Runnable: start background work; join to wait for finish.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: synchronized block: mutual exclusion on shared counter.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: ExecutorService: reuse threads; submit Callable and read Future.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Assuming thread safety without explicit synchronization strategy.
Blocking thread pools with long-running I/O operations.
Ignoring cancellation/interruption semantics in async flows.
Choosing lock primitives without contention profiling.
No observability around queue depth, latency, and rejection rates.

sections.title: Exercise - Concurrency Foundations Applied Drill
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution demonstrating **Concurrency Foundations** in a realistic scenario.

Requirements:
1. Include one baseline approach and one improvement.
2. Handle one edge/failure case.
3. Add short explanation of trade-off.
4. Keep code readable and testable.
5. Show sample output.
hints: Use explicit naming for intent clarity.
Write one failure-case test and expected result.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a thread in Java?
conceptual.answer: Independent path of execution; shares heap with other threads in same process.

conceptual.question: What is synchronized?
conceptual.answer: Intrinsic lock on object—mutual exclusion for critical sections.

conceptual.question: What is volatile?
conceptual.answer: Visibility guarantee for reads/writes—not compound atomicity for i++.

conceptual.question: What is java.util.concurrent?
conceptual.answer: Higher-level utilities: ExecutorService, ConcurrentHashMap, locks, atomics.

conceptual.question: What is ExecutorService?
conceptual.answer: Thread pool abstraction—submit tasks, manage lifecycle.

conceptual.question: What is Callable vs Runnable?
conceptual.answer: Callable returns value and throws checked exceptions—use with Future.

conceptual.question: What is CountDownLatch/CyclicBarrier?
conceptual.answer: Coordination primitives for threads meeting at points.

conceptual.question: What is deadlock?
conceptual.answer: Circular wait for locks—prevent with lock ordering and timeouts.

conceptual.question: What is race condition?
conceptual.answer: Outcome depends on timing—unsynchronized shared mutable state.

conceptual.question: What is AtomicInteger?
conceptual.answer: CAS-based atomic updates without synchronized for single variable.

conceptual.question: What is ReentrantLock?
conceptual.answer: Explicit lock with tryLock, fairness option—more flexible than synchronized.

conceptual.question: What is thread pool sizing heuristic?
What is ThreadLocal?
conceptual.answer: Per-thread variable storage—use carefully to avoid leaks in pools.

conceptual.question: What is happens-before?
conceptual.answer: JMM rule establishing visibility ordering between actions.

codeBased.question: synchronized method vs block.
codeBased.answer: Lock object scope differs—use private final lock.

codeBased.question: volatile boolean flag visibility.
codeBased.answer: Reader thread sees writer update with volatile.

codeBased.question: ScheduledExecutorService scheduleAtFixedRate.
codeBased.answer: Periodic tasks—handle exceptions or task stops.

codeBased.question: ReadWriteLock read lock many writers exclusive.

seniorScenario.question: Thread pool task queue unbounded—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: OOM from queued tasks; use bounded queues and back-pressure.

seniorScenario.question: ThreadLocal in pooled threads—leak?
seniorScenario.answer: remove() in finally—especially in app servers.

seniorScenario.question: Debugging deadlock in prod?
seniorScenario.answer: Thread dump jstack; lock ordering; timeouts.

seniorScenario.question: synchronized on Integer—bad?
seniorScenario.answer: Integer cached objects—unrelated locks; use dedicated Object.

seniorScenario.question: Virtual threads vs platform threads—when?
seniorScenario.answer: IO-bound scalability—Java 21+; CPU-bound still need cores.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed locking vs in-JVM locks?
seniorScenario.answer: Redis/etcd locks for cluster coordination—different failure modes.

wrongAnswers: volatile makes i++ atomic on an int field.
Creating a new thread per request is always the best scalability approach.
ConcurrentHashMap allows null keys like HashMap.
ExecutorService automatically shuts down when the app exits.
Thread.sleep releases locks held by the thread.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 4 Focus | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Concurrency Foundations | Explain mechanism + boundary | Syntax-only answers |
| Runtime | Performance/memory/concurrency | Mention impact explicitly | Hidden runtime costs |
| Code | Clean composable units | Prefer readability first | Over-chained complexity |
| Failure | Known pitfall + mitigation | Name one concrete case | Hand-wavy debugging |
| Interview | Decision reasoning | Compare alternatives | No trade-off discussion |
